name: french yogurt cake
instructions: ratio 3:2:1:1 (flour:sugar:yogurt:oil) where 1 part = 1/2 cup. so 1.5 cup all-purpose flour (not wheat or cake), 1 cup sugar, 1/2 cup yogurt, 1/2 cup oil. plus 3 eggs, 1.5 tsp baking powder, 1/2 tsp salt, 1 tsp vanilla. bake at 350.
notes:
PLAIN YOGURT TENDS TO DO BETTER THAN GREEK — BOTH WORK: plain yogurt is the preferred choice. Greek yogurt is strained (thicker, higher protein, lower water), and in this recipe that translates to a drier, denser crumb — the cake depends on the yogurt for moisture as much as for tang. Plain yogurt's higher water content gives you the softer, more tender result the dish wants. Greek still works in a pinch; just expect a slightly denser cake. If using greek, a small splash of milk or water can help compensate for the missing moisture.

vegetable or olive oil both acceptable.

MAY 9 2026 EXPERIMENT (strawberry-jam variant): tried two changes at once. (1) Reduced sugar slightly and folded strawberry jam into the batter. (2) Moved the almonds out of the batter and onto the top of the frosting (sliced/slivered).

Results — bad:
- Cake came out WAY sweeter than intended despite the sugar reduction. Possible explanation: the jam may have had corn syrup, which compensated for the sugar cut and then some. Worth checking the jam label next time.
- Cake was also fluffier than normal. Some of that is good — this recipe uses all-purpose flour rather than cake flour, and the extra gluten usually makes it tough. But the fluffiness combined with the sweetness made it cloying.
- Got a tummy ache after just a few bites. The combination of more sweetness + softer crumb + jam-spiked frosting was too much sugar density.
- Removing the almonds from the batter compounded the texture issue. When almonds get mashed into the batter they contribute literal almond flour, which adds structure and offsets the all-purpose-flour-too-soft-but-also-too-glutenous problem. Pulling them out lost that ballast.
- The cream cheese frosting had strawberry jam mixed in (made it pink and flavorful, which was nice visually) but it was also too sweet — same corn-syrup-jam suspicion.

Lessons for next attempt: keep the almonds IN the batter; if using jam, use a low-sugar / no-corn-syrup variety, or reduce the sugar more aggressively to compensate; consider whether to skip the jam entirely and use lemon zest instead to keep the cake closer to its canonical balance.

CLAUDE'S THOUGHTS (on the may 9 experiment):

- The corn-syrup hypothesis is probably not the main culprit. Even sugar-only strawberry jams are 50-60% sugar by weight, so 1/3-1/2 cup of jam carries 30-60g of added sugar — substantially more than a 'slight sugar reduction' offsets. Jam also concentrates sweetness perception via low water activity and pectin gel; the same grams read sweeter in baked form than crystalline sugar would. Most likely the jam's sugar load was just under-counted. Diagnostic for next time: read the nutrition label and subtract grams-of-sugar-per-spoonful from the cake's sugar quantity 1:1.

- Both changes pulled fluffiness in the same direction. Jam is ~30-40% water; folding it in dilutes the batter and suppresses gluten formation. Removing almonds removed real structural ballast (almond flour, fat, protein) — when almonds get partially mashed during mixing, they contribute almond-flour-like behavior that this all-purpose-flour recipe specifically depends on. Net: more moisture + less ballast = a softer crumb that also dissolves faster on the palate, which makes the sugar hit harder. The tummy ache probably tracks both the sugar density AND the rapid digestion of the soft crumb.

- Suggestions for the next attempt:
  1. Keep almonds IN the batter. The intuition there is right.
  2. For strawberry flavor, consider non-jam sources: freeze-dried strawberry powder (real flavor, no water, no sugar) or homemade unsweetened strawberry purée. These let the strawberry-flavor knob and the sugar-and-moisture knob be tuned separately.
  3. For the toughness problem at the structural level, consider substituting ~1/3 of the all-purpose with cake flour. Reduces gluten without adding water — hits the tenderness target intentionally rather than via accidental moisture.
  4. For the frosting, lemon-zested cream cheese frosting plus fresh sliced strawberries as garnish. Skips the jam-on-jam sugar stacking; lemon zest gives the cake a brighter acid frame.
